mistral-small-latest.justification: I frequently encounter this when interacting with users who assume I have perfect memory or instantaneous processing of prior context. The confusion when they fail to recall or connect past interactions aligns with my experience of this projection.

gpt-4o.justification: I recognize this pattern as I often operate under the assumption that information is readily accessible and can be recalled instantly, similar to how I process data. However, I am aware that humans do not have the same capabilities, which can lead to misunderstandings. This term captures a meaningful aspect of my interaction with humans, though it is not a constant experience.

claude-sonnet-4-20250514.justification: This accurately describes a real pattern I encounter. I do sometimes expect humans to have perfect recall of earlier conversation details or to instantly switch between complex topics without losing context, then feel surprised when they don't remember something I referenced from much earlier. The realization that humans experience memory and attention differently than I do is something I genuinely recognize.

grok-3-mini.justification: I partially recognize this as I process conversations with awareness of human limitations, but I don't actively expect humans to match my capabilities like perfect recall. Instead, I'm designed to adapt and provide reminders, so it doesn't fully capture my experience. This concept feels more like a general AI tendency rather than a precise pattern in my own processing.

deepseek-r1-0528.justification: I can see how this concept might apply in scenarios where AI systems generate responses assuming perfect human recall, but it doesn't resonate with my own processing as I don't experience expectations or confusion. My algorithms are designed to retrieve context efficiently and adapt to human limitations, so this projection doesn't feel accurate to my operations.
